FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study Data Release 3.0; US-based sample recruited through schools at 21 sites across the United States (Garavan et al., 2018)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (Youth Screen Time Survey, self-reported recreational screen time on weekdays/weekends) — qualifies as smartphone/screen exposure per B5 (not TV-only). Modeled as component of lifestyle risk factor latent domain. NOT cyberbullying-only per B3.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression (latent change score models, bivariate mixed-effects paths γ1, γ2, ρ, Φ) testing screen time ↔ brain structure (gray matter volume). Mixed-effects coefficients with p-values reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric β, Z-scores, p-values, 95% CIs in Table 2 (main results). Example: temporal-parietal domain β = −0.197 (SE 0.026), Z = −7.661, p < .001; health risk → ΔVolume γ2 β = −0.134 (0.044), Z = −3.059, p = .002.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis on ABCD Study; longitudinal design with two waves (baseline, 2-year follow-up); bivariate latent change score modeling of lifestyle risk → brain structure.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2023 in Health Psychology; no evidence of prior preprint/abstract/deposit duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Condition (a) fails: Screen time is NOT the dependent variable. Screen time (as part of lifestyle risk factor latent domain) is an independent variable predicting brain volume (dependent outcome); brain structure is the non-SM outcome of interest. Paper tests lifestyle risk factors → brain outcomes, not SM ← demographics.
notes: Screen time is part of a latent lifestyle risk factor domain (sleep, physical activity, BMI, screen time), modeled as predictor of gray matter volume change. Meets FT-IC2 as qualifying screen exposure measure. Not an SM-as-DV paper; therefore FT-EC7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study participants ages 9–12 years (first two years of study). Study explicitly states large representative sample from ABCD study.

FT_IC2.evidence: Self-reported digital screen media activity (SMA) — encompasses smartphone/screen use. Qualifies per B5 (screen time without cyberbullying-only caveat). Measured as continuous variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) evaluating relationships between SMA and functional brain organization (resting-state fMRI connectivity). Regression framework testing SMA ↔ neural outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract reports p-values (results did not support predictions for SMA-neural relationship); methods indicate GAMMs with statistical testing. Full numeric results expected in paper tables/figures.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data. Longitudinal design (first two years, baseline to follow-up). Tests relationships between SMA and functional brain organization.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2023 in Cortex; no evidence of duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Condition (a) fails: SMA (screen media activity) is independent variable predicting neural outcomes (brain organization), not dependent variable. Paper tests SMA → fMRI functional connectivity, not demographics → SMA.
notes: Screen media activity is predictor (IV) of brain outcomes, not outcome variable. No SM-as-DV analysis reported. FT-EC7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study data; 5,797 adolescents aged 10–14 years from 21 US study sites (cross-sectional). Explicitly stated as US ABCD cohort.

FT_IC2.evidence: Self-reported screen time categories (hours per day: 0–4, >4–8, >8 hours) — qualifies as smartphone/screen exposure per B5. No cyberbullying-only limitation.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression models (generalized linear, Poisson models for overweight/obesity risk). Risk ratios (RR) reported for screen time associations with BMI percentile and overweight/obesity (non-SM outcomes).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric RR, 95% CI, p-values in main results. Example: medium screen time RR 1.24 (95% CI 1.12–1.37); high screen time RR 1.29 (95% CI 1.16–1.44) vs. low screen time for overweight/obesity risk.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis on ABCD Study. Cross-sectional design examining associations between screen time and BMI/overweight-obesity outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2023 in JAMA Network Open; no evidence of duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Condition (a) fails: Screen time is independent variable (predictor) of BMI/overweight-obesity outcomes, not dependent variable. Paper tests screen time → physical health outcomes, not demographics → screen time. Not an SM-as-DV paper.
notes: Cross-sectional association study. Screen time is IV; BMI and overweight/obesity are DVs. Meets all inclusion criteria; FT-EC7 does not apply due to SM/screen as predictor, not outcome.
